_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and clinical management per Bisoffi PLoS Negl Trop Dis 2013 (PMID 23671922) and Buonfrate Lancet Infect Dis 2019 (PMID 31427178). Ivermectin treatment efficacy per Henriquez-Camacho Cochrane 2016 (PMID 26778150). Hyperinfection in immunosuppression per Keiser & Nutman Clin Microbiol Rev 2004 (PMID 14702094). HTLV-1 coinfection per Carvalho Acta Trop 2004 PMID 14747251. Pre-immunosuppression screening per CDC and WHO 2024 NTD elimination targets. Diagnostic test characteristics per Bisoffi 2014 PLoS Negl Trop Dis (PMID 24675118). Global burden estimate 614 million per Buonfrate Pathogens 2020 (PMID 32486198).

h1Title: Strongyloidiasis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Strongyloidiasis is a parasitic infection of the small intestine caused by the nematode Strongyloides stercoralis, which uniquely completes its life cycle within a single human host through autoinfection — allowing decades of asymptomatic carriage interrupted by life-threatening hyperinfection if the host becomes immunosuppressed. Global estimates indicate 600-614 million people are infected, concentrated in tropical and subtropical regions but persisting in immigrant communities and the rural southeast United States. Chronic infection produces intermittent abdominal pain, diarrhea, urticaria, and a serpiginous moving rash (larva currens); peripheral eosinophilia is present in 70-80% of cases. Hyperinfection syndrome triggered by corticosteroids carries mortality above 60% without prompt ivermectin therapy. A single oral dose of ivermectin 200 µg/kg cures more than 80% of uncomplicated infections; pre-immunosuppression screening of all patients from endemic regions is mandatory.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 600-614 million people infected globally (Buonfrate Pathogens 2020); seroprevalence 30-60% in some endemic communities; 1-4% in southern Europe migrants; 1-3% in rural southeastern United States
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; men over 40 with occupational soil exposure at elevated risk; HTLV-1 co-infection increases hyperinfection risk 10-fold; immunocompromised patients (corticosteroids, transplant, hematologic malignancy) at greatest risk for fatal disease
keyStats.avgAge: Acute infection at any age; chronic carriage may persist 40+ years through autoinfection; hyperinfection typically in older immunosuppressed adults
keyStats.globalCases: WHO designates strongyloidiasis as a neglected tropical disease; case-fatality of disseminated hyperinfection above 60% without treatment; mortality 15-30% with timely ivermectin

definition: Strongyloidiasis (ICD-10: B78) is human infection by Strongyloides stercoralis, a small intestinal nematode 2-3 mm in length whose unique life cycle includes parthenogenetic reproduction, internal autoinfection, and the capacity for hyperinfection in immunocompromised hosts. Larvae penetrate intact skin from contaminated soil, migrate via lungs to the small intestine where parthenogenetic females embed in the mucosa and release eggs. Eggs hatch in the intestine to release rhabditiform larvae; some are excreted in feces (continuing the environmental cycle), while others transform into filariform larvae that penetrate the perianal skin or colonic mucosa and re-enter the circulation (autoinfection). This autoinfection cycle allows the infection to persist indefinitely in a single host — patients exposed in childhood have presented with hyperinfection 50-70 years later. Strongyloidiasis is classified into acute infection (within weeks of exposure), chronic uncomplicated infection (the dominant form, often asymptomatic or with intermittent gastrointestinal and dermatological symptoms over decades), hyperinfection syndrome (massive intra-host proliferation, usually triggered by corticosteroids or HTLV-1 co-infection), and disseminated strongyloidiasis (larvae spread beyond the gastrointestinal tract and lungs to liver, brain, kidneys, and other organs). Management is led by infectious disease, tropical medicine, and gastroenterology, with pre-immunosuppression screening managed across oncology, transplant medicine, and rheumatology.

typesClassification.description: Initial 2-3 week period after skin penetration. Local irritation at entry site, pulmonary symptoms during larval migration, and gastrointestinal symptoms as adult worms establish. Rarely diagnosed because symptoms are non-specific and exposure may not be recognized.

Months to decades of asymptomatic or intermittently symptomatic carriage. Eosinophilia present in 70-80% of cases. Periodic dermatologic (urticaria, larva currens) and gastrointestinal symptoms. Confirmed by serology, repeated stool examination, or agar plate culture.

Massively accelerated autoinfection following immunosuppression, particularly corticosteroids in any dose and HTLV-1 co-infection. Severe gastrointestinal symptoms, eosinophilia paradoxically absent or low, fever, and respiratory failure. Mortality above 60% without prompt ivermectin.

Larvae spread beyond gastrointestinal tract and lungs to liver, brain, kidneys, and other organs. Severe gram-negative bacteremia and meningitis (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus) commonly accompany dissemination, with mortality 70-90% even with treatment.

Pathognomonic serpiginous, intensely pruritic rash advancing at 5-15 cm per hour on the trunk, perianal area, and buttocks as larvae migrate beneath the skin. Distinguishes strongyloidiasis from other cutaneous larva migrans (which advances at millimeters per day).

primarySymptoms: Recurrent intermittent abdominal pain, bloating, nausea, and diarrhea (sometimes alternating with constipation) that persists or recurs over months to years.
Intensely pruritic, serpiginous, advancing rash on the trunk, perianal area, or buttocks (larva currens) that moves visibly at 5-15 cm per hour and resolves within 12-48 hours.
Recurrent urticaria and chronic itching, particularly on the trunk and limbs, often confused with idiopathic chronic urticaria.
Asthma-like cough and wheeze during larval migration through the lungs, frequently responsive to bronchodilators but recurring without antiparasitic therapy.
Peripheral blood eosinophilia, often above 1,000/µL, persisting for months in chronic infection and a useful screening trigger for tropical medicine evaluation.
Weight loss and malabsorption with steatorrhea in heavy or chronic infection, particularly in children in endemic regions.
Severe abdominal pain, profuse watery diarrhea, intestinal obstruction, gastrointestinal bleeding, and ileus in hyperinfection.
Hemoptysis, respiratory failure, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates in pulmonary hyperinfection or disseminated disease.
Bacterial meningitis or sepsis with gram-negative or polymicrobial organisms transported by migrating larvae — present in 40-80% of disseminated cases.
Asymptomatic eosinophilia in a returning traveler or immigrant from an endemic region — the most common presentation triggering screening.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent eosinophilia above 500/µL in any patient from an endemic region or with relevant travel history
Recurrent unexplained urticaria or itching, particularly perianal or truncal
Recurrent gastrointestinal symptoms after travel to or migration from tropical regions
Mild gastrointestinal upset or asthma symptoms in a patient about to start corticosteroids or other immunosuppression
Visible serpiginous rash that disappears within hours to days
emergencySigns: Severe, watery diarrhea with abdominal distention, vomiting, and hypotension in a patient on corticosteroids or other immunosuppression — possible hyperinfection requiring urgent ivermectin and parenteral antibiotics
Acute respiratory failure with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, hemoptysis, and fever in a strongyloidiasis-endemic patient — pulmonary hyperinfection
Gram-negative or polymicrobial bacteremia, meningitis, or sepsis in an immunocompromised patient with travel or migration history — exclude disseminated strongyloidiasis
Sudden hemorrhagic gastritis, hematochezia, or massive gastrointestinal bleeding in a patient with eosinophilia and immunosuppression
Skin purpura, particularly periumbilical 'thumbprint' pattern, in an immunosuppressed patient — pathognomonic for disseminated strongyloidiasis

causes.cause: Strongyloides stercoralis larvae from contaminated soil
causes.description: Filariform larvae in warm moist soil contaminated with human feces penetrate intact skin during walking barefoot, gardening, agricultural work, or wading through contaminated water. Migration via venous return reaches lungs, then small intestine, where adult parthenogenetic females embed in the mucosa within 2-4 weeks.

causes.cause: Autoinfection cycle within a single host
causes.description: Rhabditiform larvae in the intestine transform into infective filariform larvae that penetrate the perianal skin or colonic mucosa and re-enter the circulation, perpetuating infection without further environmental exposure. This unique mechanism allows decades-long carriage and progression to hyperinfection when host immunity is suppressed.

causes.cause: Corticosteroid use in chronically infected patients
causes.description: Even short courses of corticosteroids (oral prednisolone, IV methylprednisolone, intra-articular injection) can trigger massive autoinfection acceleration and hyperinfection syndrome. The mechanism involves direct inhibition of larva-killing eosinophils and altered cortisol-like host factors that promote larval molting. Strongyloides is the most clinically important reason to screen for parasitic infection before corticosteroid therapy in at-risk patients.

Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 infection impairs Th2 immunity essential for controlling Strongyloides and raises hyperinfection risk approximately 10-fold. Co-infection is common in some Caribbean and Japanese populations and warrants HTLV-1 serology in any patient with severe strongyloidiasis.

Solid-organ transplantation, hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation, hematologic malignancy (especially CLL, lymphoma), advanced HIV (in some settings), and biological agents targeting Th2 immunity raise hyperinfection risk. Universal screening of at-risk patients before transplantation is recommended.

Because of autoinfection, infected individuals can carry the parasite for decades after leaving an endemic region. Cases have been documented in World War II veterans 50-70 years after presumed exposure. This makes a remote travel or residence history relevant in any patient presenting with compatible symptoms or eosinophilia.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or extended travel in tropical or subtropical regions
riskFactors.description: Endemic regions include Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, the Caribbean, Brazil, parts of southern Europe, Aboriginal communities in Australia, and the rural southeastern United States. Seroprevalence reaches 30-60% in some communities.

Agricultural workers, gardeners, miners, plumbers, and military personnel exposed to warm moist soil contaminated with human feces have elevated risk. Use of footwear and improved sanitation reduces incidence.

Even short courses (a few doses) can trigger hyperinfection in chronic carriers. Single intra-articular injection has been documented to cause hyperinfection. All at-risk patients should be screened with serology before starting corticosteroids.

Coinfection raises hyperinfection risk 10-fold and reduces ivermectin cure rates by approximately 30%. HTLV-1 serology recommended in any patient with severe or refractory strongyloidiasis from at-risk populations.
Universal pre-transplant screening with serology recommended for all candidates from endemic regions; positive cases treated with ivermectin before immunosuppression starts.
B-cell malignancies and aggressive chemotherapy regimens impair immune surveillance against Strongyloides. Screening before chemotherapy in patients from endemic regions is reasonable.
Reported hyperinfection cases are more common in men over 40, possibly reflecting cumulative occupational exposure and a higher proportion seeking medical attention.

affectedDemographics: Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Lao PDR, Thailand, Vietnam): seroprevalence 20-40% in some rural communities.
Sub-Saharan Africa: highly endemic in many regions; co-endemic with HIV and other parasites.
Caribbean and Brazil: moderate to high prevalence; HTLV-1 co-endemicity raises severe disease risk.
Rural southeastern United States: residual prevalence 1-3% in rural Appalachia, Mississippi delta, and Puerto Rico, persisting in older adults.
Migrant and refugee populations in high-income countries: 1-25% seroprevalence depending on origin; universal screening recommended for refugees from sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia.
Returning travelers from endemic regions: 1-5% in long-term aid workers, missionaries, and military veterans.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is challenging because conventional stool microscopy has low sensitivity (20-50% even with multiple samples) due to intermittent and low-level larval excretion. Serology by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is the most sensitive test (85-95%) and is the first-line screening modality for at-risk patients, particularly before immunosuppression. The Bordier and InBios assays are commonly used; cross-reactivity with other helminth infections (filariasis, schistosomiasis) can produce false positives in heavily endemic settings. Polymerase chain reaction on stool is highly specific and increasingly available in reference laboratories. Microscopic methods include direct stool examination, formalin-ethyl acetate concentration, Baermann technique (sensitivity 70-80% with proper technique), and agar plate culture (sensitivity 90-95% — currently the most sensitive parasitological method). Agar plate culture requires fresh stool and a 1-3 day incubation. Multiple stool samples on consecutive days improve sensitivity for any microscopic method. In hyperinfection, larvae are typically abundant in stool, sputum, and bronchoalveolar lavage; eosinophilia may be paradoxically absent because of corticosteroid immunosuppression — a dangerous diagnostic pitfall. Peripheral blood eosinophilia above 500/µL in any patient from an endemic region warrants stool studies and serology. Imaging (CT abdomen showing thickened bowel, paralytic ileus, mesenteric edema) and endoscopy (with biopsy demonstrating larvae in duodenal mucosa) are reserved for severe cases. HTLV-1 serology is recommended in any patient with severe or refractory disease. WHO and CDC recommend universal serologic screening of patients from endemic regions before solid-organ transplantation, hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation, or planned long-term corticosteroid therapy.

diagnosticTests.test: Strongyloides serology (ELISA, Luminex, or rapid test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening with 85-95% sensitivity and good specificity; recommended before immunosuppression in at-risk patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 3-7 days. Cross-reactivity with other helminths possible; confirmatory tests recommended in high-burden regions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive parasitological method (90-95%); detects larval tracks crawling out of stool on agar with a bacterial 'serpentine'
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Submit fresh stool sample to the lab; results read at 24-72 hours. Available in reference laboratories.

diagnosticTests.test: Direct stool microscopy with formalin-ethyl acetate concentration
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects rhabditiform larvae in stool; sensitivity 20-50% per sample, rising with multiple samples
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Provide stool samples on three consecutive days for best yield. Result available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Uses larval thermotropism to detect Strongyloides in stool; sensitivity 70-80% with optimal technique
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Reference laboratory procedure; result in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highly specific (>95%) with rising sensitivity in modern assays; not yet routinely available outside reference labs
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Submit stool sample; results in 2-7 days depending on lab capacity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Eosinophilia above 500/µL is present in 70-80% of chronic infection; absence in immunosuppressed hyperinfection is a danger signal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine complete blood count with differential; immediate result.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies co-infection that increases hyperinfection risk 10-fold and reduces ivermectin cure rates by 30%
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 5-10 days. Recommended in patients with severe disease from at-risk populations.

diagnosticTests.test: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsy (severe cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates larvae embedded in duodenal mucosa with characteristic clinicopathologic features of mucosal eosinophilia and Charcot-Leyden crystals
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure under sedation; biopsy results in 2-7 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is oral ivermectin in all forms of strongyloidiasis. For uncomplicated chronic infection, a single dose of ivermectin 200 µg/kg taken with food cures more than 80% of patients in randomized trials; a repeat dose at 2 weeks raises cure rates above 95% by capturing larvae that have completed an autoinfection cycle since the first dose. Two-dose regimens are now recommended in many guidelines including the recent Cochrane 2016 review. Albendazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days is an alternative but is less effective (60-75% cure) and is generally reserved for ivermectin-intolerant patients or in combination with ivermectin in hyperinfection. Hyperinfection and disseminated disease require daily oral ivermectin 200 µg/kg continued until stool and sputum are larva-negative for 2 weeks, plus broad-spectrum antibiotics (including coverage for gram-negative bacteria and Enterococcus) because of high rates of associated bacteremia. Subcutaneous and rectal ivermectin formulations are used in patients with paralytic ileus who cannot tolerate oral therapy (compassionate use). Concurrent corticosteroid dose reduction is essential when feasible. HTLV-1-coinfected patients have reduced ivermectin cure rates and may need repeated courses or maintenance therapy. Pre-immunosuppression screening of all patients from endemic regions with Strongyloides serology is the single most important preventive intervention; positive cases should receive ivermectin treatment before starting corticosteroids, chemotherapy, or transplant immunosuppression. Test-of-cure with serology shows declining titers over 6-12 months; persistent positive eosinophilia or symptoms warrant repeat treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin (200 µg/kg single oral dose, repeated at 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for uncomplicated chronic strongyloidiasis. Take with food for improved absorption. Side effects include mild headache, abdominal pain, and transient pruritus. Contraindicated in children under 15 kg and during the first trimester of pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single dose cures 80-85%; two doses 14 days apart cure 95% (Henriquez-Camacho Cochrane 2016)

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin daily until larva-negative (hyperinfection)
medicalTreatments.description: Daily oral ivermectin 200 µg/kg until stool and sputum are larva-negative for at least 14 days. In ileus, subcutaneous (compassionate use) or rectal formulations may be needed. Combined with broad-spectrum antibiotics for associated bacteremia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduced from above 60% to 15-30% with prompt therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative when ivermectin is contraindicated or unavailable. Less effective than ivermectin against Strongyloides. Side-effects: hepatic enzyme elevation, headache, rare leukopenia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 60-75% as monotherapy; useful adjunct to ivermectin in hyperinfection

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem, or ceftriaxone plus aminoglycoside) for hyperinfection-associated bacteremia
medicalTreatments.description: Treats gram-negative and polymicrobial bacteremia and meningitis transported by migrating larvae. Adjusted by culture results once available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality of disseminated disease by 30-50% when initiated early

medicalTreatments.name: Reduction or discontinuation of corticosteroids and other immunosuppressants where feasible
medicalTreatments.description: Tapering or stopping iatrogenic immunosuppression allows host immunity to control infection. Coordinated with the team managing the underlying condition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves cure rates in hyperinfection and reduces relapse risk

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-immunosuppression screening of at-risk patients with serology and pre-emptive ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: Universal serologic screening of patients from endemic regions before solid-organ transplant, stem-cell transplant, chemotherapy, or planned long-term corticosteroid therapy; positive cases treated with ivermectin before starting immunosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eliminates hyperinfection risk in identified patients; recommended by CDC, WHO, and major transplant societies

whySeeSpecialist: Tropical medicine and infectious disease specialists confirm diagnosis, choose appropriate therapy, exclude HTLV-1 co-infection, and manage complications. Hyperinfection and disseminated disease require intensive care and infectious disease guidance. Pre-immunosuppression screening of patients from endemic regions should be coordinated between transplant medicine, oncology, rheumatology, and infectious disease teams.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist familiar with Strongyloides serology and modern ivermectin dosing. For pre-transplant or pre-chemotherapy screening, a dedicated tropical medicine clinic or infectious disease consult service is ideal. Hyperinfection demands rapid escalation to a center with parenteral ivermectin access and infectious disease, gastroenterology, pulmonology, and intensive care capability.
hospitalCriteria: Infectious disease and tropical medicine clinic with Strongyloides serology and stool agar culture
Access to ivermectin (oral and, in selected cases, subcutaneous or rectal compassionate use)
Endoscopy service for biopsy in severe disease
Multidisciplinary screening pathway for transplant and oncology patients
Microbiology laboratory familiar with parasite identification
Outpatient follow-up clinic for repeat serology and clinical assessment
keyFacilities: Infectious disease outpatient and inpatient services
Oncology and rheumatology services with screening protocols
preventionStrategies: Wear footwear in endemic regions to prevent skin penetration by filariform larvae in contaminated soil.
Improve sanitation infrastructure (latrines, sewage management) and access to clean water in endemic communities.
Screen all patients from endemic regions with Strongyloides serology before initiating corticosteroids, chemotherapy, transplant immunosuppression, or biologics.
Treat seropositive patients pre-emptively with ivermectin before starting immunosuppression.
Educate travelers, missionaries, military personnel, and aid workers on avoiding bare-foot walking in tropical regions.
Maintain accurate travel and residence history in all patients with unexplained eosinophilia.
Implement universal refugee screening protocols where local epidemiology supports it.
lifestyleModifications: Take ivermectin with food to improve absorption and tolerability.
Maintain accurate medication and travel-history records to share with all healthcare providers.
Wear footwear in any potentially contaminated environment, particularly in tropical regions.
Disclose remote travel or residence history (even decades earlier) to clinicians evaluating eosinophilia, unexplained urticaria, or new gastrointestinal symptoms.
Follow up for repeat serology at 6-12 months after treatment to confirm declining titers.
Avoid self-administered corticosteroids (e.g., over-the-counter creams, oral steroids from other countries) without medical guidance if prior strongyloidiasis is suspected.

recommended: Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) and balanced calorie intake to support recovery from chronic infection and weight loss
Iron- and folate-rich foods to address coexistent nutritional deficiencies common in tropical settings
Hydration with 1.5-2 L of water daily during ivermectin therapy
Vitamin and mineral repletion in malnourished patients particularly in pediatric and immigrant populations
avoid: Self-administered herbal preparations marketed as parasite cleanses without medical supervision
Unsupervised corticosteroid use including topical steroids for unexplained rashes in patients from endemic regions
Untreated drinking water and unsafe food preparation in endemic regions

exerciseGuidelines: Mild to moderate aerobic activity is safe during and after treatment. Patients with hyperinfection or severe disease should follow a graded return to activity guided by the treating clinician. There are no specific restrictions for fully treated chronic strongyloidiasis patients.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed ivermectin doses on schedule and with food
Monitor for symptom resolution and persistence of eosinophilia
Report any new abdominal symptoms, breathlessness, or rash promptly
Use footwear and avoid bare-soil contact in any endemic environment
Disclose strongyloidiasis history to all clinicians, particularly before any immunosuppressive therapy
Attend follow-up serology testing at 6 and 12 months to document treatment response
prognosis: Outlook depends on disease stage and host immunity. Uncomplicated chronic strongyloidiasis cures in over 95% of patients with two doses of ivermectin 14 days apart, with full resolution of eosinophilia and symptoms within 3-6 months. Serologic titers fall by 50% within 6-12 months and usually become negative within 18 months in successfully treated cases. Hyperinfection syndrome carries a mortality of 60-85% without treatment; with prompt ivermectin and broad-spectrum antibiotics, mortality falls to 15-30% but remains high in elderly and critically ill patients. Disseminated strongyloidiasis with bacteremia or meningitis has a mortality of 70-90% even with intensive treatment. HTLV-1 co-infected patients have a reduced ivermectin cure rate of approximately 50-70% and may require repeated courses or maintenance therapy. Without pre-immunosuppression screening, the lifetime risk of fatal hyperinfection in an undiagnosed carrier exposed to corticosteroids is substantial; the case for universal screening of at-risk patients is therefore very strong.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom resolution within 1-4 weeks of ivermectin therapy in uncomplicated chronic disease. Peripheral eosinophilia normalizes over 3-6 months. Serologic titers decline over 6-12 months, usually becoming negative by 18 months. Hyperinfection: recovery takes 2-6 weeks of inpatient care; long-term residual deficits depend on extent of organ involvement.
complications: Hyperinfection syndrome triggered by corticosteroids or immunosuppression — mortality 60-85% without prompt ivermectin therapy.
Disseminated strongyloidiasis with gram-negative or polymicrobial bacteremia and meningitis — mortality 70-90%.
Chronic malabsorption, weight loss, failure to thrive, and growth retardation in children with heavy chronic infection.
Recurrent urticaria, larva currens, and chronic skin symptoms affecting quality of life over decades.
Asthma-like respiratory disease during chronic larval migration; may be confused with allergic asthma.
Ivermectin-related neurological toxicity in patients with concomitant filariasis (Loa loa) — pre-treatment screening required in co-endemic regions.

supportResources.name: CDC — Strongyloidiasis Resources
supportResources.description: US Centers for Disease Control patient and clinician resources on diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of strongyloidiasis.

supportResources.name: WHO — Soil-Transmitted Helminthiases
supportResources.description: WHO information on global burden, public health control, and treatment for soil-transmitted helminth infections including strongyloidiasis.

supportResources.name: American Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene (ASTMH)
supportResources.description: Professional society publishing clinical guidelines and educational resources for tropical and parasitic diseases.

supportResources.name: MSF Médecins Sans Frontières — Clinical Guidelines
supportResources.description: Field-tested treatment recommendations for parasitic infections in low-resource settings, including strongyloidiasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause eosinophilia in tropical-exposure patients. Schistosomiasis produces hematuria (S. haematobium) or bloody diarrhea with hepatosplenic fibrosis (S. mansoni, japonicum); diagnosis is by stool or urine egg microscopy and treatment is praziquantel. Strongyloidiasis produces serpiginous larva currens, urticaria, and characteristic autoinfection-driven persistence; treatment is ivermectin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hookworm Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hookworm and Strongyloides share skin penetration and lung migration. Hookworm causes iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss in the intestine and is diagnosed by characteristic eggs on stool microscopy; treated with albendazole or mebendazole. Strongyloides lacks anemia, has autoinfection capability, larva currens rash, and requires ivermectin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma produces episodic airflow obstruction and responds reversibly to bronchodilators. Strongyloides pulmonary migration causes asthma-like symptoms with persistent eosinophilia; chronic 'asthma' resistant to therapy in patients from endemic regions warrants Strongyloides screening.

confusedWithConditions.name: Idiopathic Chronic Urticaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Idiopathic chronic urticaria is non-infectious recurring urticaria without identifiable trigger. Strongyloides-associated urticaria may have a serpiginous larva currens component, peripheral eosinophilia, and recurrent gastrointestinal symptoms — features that should prompt serologic screening in any patient from an endemic region.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic tropical helminth causing eosinophilia; both warrant screening in returning travelers and migrants.

relatedConditions.name: Hookworm Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Soil-transmitted helminth with overlapping geography and transmission mode; differentiated by clinical features and stool studies.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphatic Filariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another tropical helminth requiring pre-treatment screening before ivermectin therapy to avoid encephalopathy in Loa loa co-infection.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic with strongyloidiasis; both must be considered in febrile travelers and migrants from tropical regions.

faqs.question: What is strongyloidiasis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Strongyloidiasis is a parasitic infection of the small intestine caused by the nematode Strongyloides stercoralis. The parasite can live in the body for decades through self-reinfection. Steroid or other immunosuppressant therapy can trigger life-threatening hyperinfection. Treatment is a short course of oral ivermectin.

faqs.question: How do you get strongyloidiasis?
faqs.answer: Infection occurs when filariform larvae in warm, moist soil contaminated with human feces penetrate intact skin — typically through bare feet, gardening, or wading in contaminated water. Migration through lungs and small intestine completes the cycle. Person-to-person spread requires direct contact with infected stool.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of strongyloidiasis?
faqs.answer: Chronic infection often causes intermittent abdominal pain, diarrhea, urticaria, and a serpiginous moving rash (larva currens) on the trunk. Many patients are asymptomatic with peripheral eosinophilia. Hyperinfection causes severe diarrhea, respiratory failure, and bacteremia, with mortality above 60% without ivermectin.

faqs.question: How is strongyloidiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Serology (ELISA) is the most sensitive test (85-95%). Stool agar plate culture and Baermann technique improve parasite recovery. Direct stool microscopy has limited sensitivity (20-50%). PCR is highly specific. Peripheral eosinophilia is present in 70-80% of chronic cases.

faqs.question: Can strongyloidiasis kill you?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hyperinfection triggered by corticosteroids carries 60-85% mortality without treatment; disseminated disease with bacteremia has 70-90% mortality. With prompt ivermectin and broad-spectrum antibiotics, mortality falls to 15-30% but remains substantial in critically ill patients.

faqs.question: How is strongyloidiasis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is oral ivermectin 200 µg/kg as a single dose, repeated at 14 days, which cures over 95% of uncomplicated infections. Hyperinfection requires daily ivermectin until larva-negative for 2 weeks plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Albendazole is a second-line alternative.

faqs.question: Why do steroids cause hyperinfection?
faqs.answer: Corticosteroids suppress eosinophils and the Th2 immune response that normally controls Strongyloides, allowing massive larval autoinfection and dissemination. Even short courses (a few oral doses or single intra-articular injection) can trigger hyperinfection. Pre-treatment screening is essential.

faqs.question: Should I be screened before starting steroids?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you have lived in or traveled extensively to a tropical or subtropical region — even decades ago. Strongyloides serology before starting corticosteroids, chemotherapy, transplant immunosuppression, or biologic agents is recommended by CDC, WHO, and major transplant societies.

faqs.question: Can strongyloidiasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is uncommon after a complete two-dose course of ivermectin (cure rate above 95%). Persistent eosinophilia or symptoms beyond 3-6 months warrant repeat serology and a second treatment course. Serologic titers normally fall to negative within 12-18 months.

faqs.question: What is larva currens?
faqs.answer: Larva currens is a pathognomonic intensely pruritic, serpiginous rash that advances at 5-15 cm per hour on the trunk, perianal area, or buttocks as filariform larvae migrate beneath the skin. It distinguishes Strongyloides from other cutaneous larva migrans (which moves at millimeters per day).

faqs.question: How long can strongyloidiasis stay in the body?
faqs.answer: Decades. Because Strongyloides can complete its life cycle within a single host through autoinfection, infections acquired in childhood can persist for 50-70 years and present with hyperinfection when immunosuppression is given. WWII veterans have presented decades after presumed exposure.

faqs.question: Is ivermectin safe?
faqs.answer: Ivermectin is well-tolerated in most patients at therapeutic doses. Common side effects include mild headache, abdominal pain, and transient pruritus. It is contraindicated in children under 15 kg and the first trimester of pregnancy. Patients with Loa loa co-infection require pre-screening to avoid encephalopathy.

faqs.question: Is strongyloidiasis contagious?
faqs.answer: Person-to-person transmission requires direct contact with stool containing infective filariform larvae and is uncommon under normal hygiene. Outbreaks in long-term care facilities and households with poor sanitation have been reported. Sexual transmission through anal intercourse is theoretically possible.

faqs.question: Where is strongyloidiasis common?
faqs.answer: Strongyloidiasis is widespread across tropical and subtropical regions: Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, the Caribbean, Brazil, parts of southern Europe, Aboriginal communities in Australia, and the rural southeastern United States. Seroprevalence reaches 30-60% in some endemic communities.

faqs.question: Can children get strongyloidiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in endemic regions can develop chronic strongyloidiasis with malabsorption, weight loss, and growth retardation. Ivermectin is approved in children over 15 kg; in smaller children, albendazole is an alternative. Screening of refugee and immigrant children is recommended.

faqs.question: Does eosinophilia always mean parasitic infection?
faqs.answer: No, but eosinophilia above 500/µL in a patient from a tropical region or with travel exposure raises high suspicion. Other causes include atopy, allergic rhinitis, drug reactions, malignancy, and autoimmune disease. Stool studies and Strongyloides serology are reasonable first-line tests.

faqs.question: What is HTLV-1 co-infection?
faqs.answer: Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 impairs Th2 immunity and increases hyperinfection risk approximately 10-fold while reducing ivermectin cure rate by 30%. HTLV-1 serology is recommended in any patient with severe or refractory strongyloidiasis from at-risk populations.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women be treated?
faqs.answer: Ivermectin is generally avoided in the first trimester. In severe maternal disease threatening life, individualized risk-benefit assessment may justify treatment after the first trimester. Mild chronic infection is typically deferred to postpartum treatment with breastfeeding maintained.

faqs.question: How does strongyloidiasis cause sepsis?
faqs.answer: During hyperinfection or disseminated disease, migrating filariform larvae transport gut bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus) through bowel wall and into the bloodstream, causing gram-negative or polymicrobial bacteremia and meningitis. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are mandatory alongside ivermectin.

faqs.question: Can strongyloidiasis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention includes wearing footwear in endemic regions, improved sanitation, and avoiding bare-soil contact. Universal serologic screening of patients from endemic regions before immunosuppression — and pre-emptive ivermectin treatment of positive cases — eliminates hyperinfection risk.

faqs.question: How do I confirm cure after treatment?
faqs.answer: Cure is confirmed by symptom resolution, normalization of peripheral eosinophilia within 3-6 months, and declining serologic titers (50% fall by 6-12 months, negative by 18 months). Persistent symptoms or eosinophilia after 6 months warrants repeat stool studies and a second ivermectin course.

metaDescription: Strongyloidiasis: Strongyloides parasitic infection with hyperinfection risk on steroids. Symptoms, ivermectin treatment. Medically reviewed tropical guide.
